Q1: I kept things pretty simple and consistent throughout the course. I usually studied in the mornings before my part-time job, maybe two or three hours at a time, and then a bit more on weekends. I'd go through the week's materials in order, watch the videos, take some notes, and then move on. It wasn't exciting, but it worked for me and kept me from falling behind.
Q2: I definitely tried to stick to regular times each week because that's just how I function best. Mondays and Wednesdays were my main study mornings, and Sundays I'd catch up on anything I missed. Sometimes deadlines would push me to add extra hours, but honestly, I tried to avoid that by staying on schedule. I don't like the stress of last-minute cramming.
Q3: Usually I logged in because it was just part of my morning routine, like checking what's due this week or if there's new content. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline or a discussion post, and that would remind me to check in. It became almost automatic after a few weeks, like brushing my teeth before studying.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it just became habit. I'd wake up, make my tea, and open the LMS while eating breakfast to see what was new. I think what helped was doing it at the same time every day so I didn't have to think about it. It wasn't particularly exciting, but it felt normal and kept me on track.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them while taking notes, sometimes replaying parts I didn't catch the first time. The discussion boards were also helpful because reading what other students thought helped me understand different perspectives. I watched a lot of video content, probably close to five hours total.
Q6: I mostly ignored the extra reading materials and supplementary PDFs, to be honest. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my phone was tiring. I figured if I understood the videos and did the assignments, I'd be okay. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped because they felt repetitive.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or if it helped clarify something confusing. I appreciated when things were straightforward and didn't waste time with unnecessary complexity. If I could see how it would help my grade or understanding, I'd do it. Otherwise, I'd rather spend my limited time on what matters most.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like extras I could skip if I was tired or busy, which happened a lot. Required ones I'd do no matter what because I knew they affected my grade. I'd sometimes feel guilty skipping optional stuff, but then I'd remind myself I was already keeping up with the main work. It's hard to do everything when you're managing other responsibilities.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download the lecture slides and audio when I had wifi, then review them on my commute to work or during breaks. I also made handwritten notes that I'd study from instead of going back to the LMS. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just me and my notebook.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my notes or listening to downloaded lectures on my phone while on the bus. The LMS wouldn't know I was studying because I wasn't logged in. I'd also discuss concepts with classmates in person or over the phone, which helped me understand things better but left no digital trace.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once online, take detailed notes, and then never go back to it on the LMS. My notes became my main study material. Sometimes I'd download PDFs and read them later on my phone when I had time. The LMS only saw that one access, but I studied those materials multiple times.
Q12: There were weeks when I felt really bored with the material, like it was just going through the motions. When the content felt repetitive or too easy, I'd lose interest and just do the minimum. Also, when I was exhausted from work or dealing with money stress, it was harder to focus even when I logged in.
Q13: Definitely. When I had extra shifts at work or was worried about paying bills, the LMS became just another task to check off rather than something I engaged with deeply. Fatigue made me rush through videos faster than I should have. Sometimes I'd log in, see how much there was to do, and feel overwhelmed even though I was technically keeping up.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely, thinking about work or personal stuff. I'd realize I'd watched ten minutes without absorbing anything and have to rewind. Sometimes I'd click through discussion posts without really reading them just to feel like I'd done something. It looked like engagement but wasn't really.
Q15: I'd sometimes put off the longer readings or more complex assignments because they felt like too much effort when I was tired. I knew they were important, but I'd tell myself I'd do them later when I had more energy. Usually I'd get them done eventually, but not always with my best effort. Procrastination happened when things felt boring or overwhelming.
Q16: Deadlines were my main motivator, honestly. I'd plan my week around when things were due and make sure I submitted everything on time. The days before a deadline I'd be more active on the LMS, double-checking requirements and finishing up work. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have been as consistent because the routine needs that structure.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, I'd read it carefully and try to apply it to the next one. Good feedback motivated me to keep doing what I was doing, while critical feedback made me pay more attention to those areas. I appreciated when instructors gave specific comments because it showed they actually looked at my work. It made the effort feel worthwhile.
Q18: The weekly structure really helped me stay organized because I knew exactly what to expect each week. Having clear modules with videos, readings, and assignments in order made it easy to follow along. I liked that I could plan my routine around the course schedule. If it had been more chaotic or unpredictable, I would have struggled more.
Q19: The video format made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when there were too many supplementary materials that felt optional but also important. Sometimes the discussion requirements felt forced, like I was posting just to meet a quota rather than because I had something meaningful to say. Clearer priorities would have helped.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring or trying to figure things out without actually completing work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting or don't feel confident in their understanding yet. Or they could be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to finish things even when they're trying. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me in some ways. I studied a lot offline with my notes and downloaded materials, so my LMS activity didn't fully reflect my effort. If someone's efficient and learns well independently, they might not need to be on the platform constantly. Performance comes from understanding, not just clicking around online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and whether you submit things on time. But they miss the quality of attention, offline studying, and real understanding. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They show behavior but not the thinking behind it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious about what you're learning and want to do well, not just pass. For me, engagement is showing up consistently, asking questions when confused, and applying feedback. It's about being mentally present, not just physically logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from my handwritten notes or how often I discussed concepts with classmates in person. It also wouldn't capture that I was sometimes bored but still disciplined enough to keep going. My mindfulness practice helped me manage stress, but that internal work doesn't show up anywhere. The data misses the human side of learning.
